items.title: Building AI Systems at Scale
items.excerpt: Lessons learned from deploying ML models that serve millions of requests...
items.author: Smrita Pandey
items.content:
## Introduction

When deploying machine learning models in production environments, the challenges extend far beyond model accuracy. In this post, I'll share lessons learned from building and scaling AI systems that serve millions of users.

## Key Considerations

### 1. Infrastructure

The foundation of any scalable AI system is robust infrastructure. This includes:

- **Model serving**: Using optimized inference servers
- **Auto-scaling**: Dynamic resource allocation based on demand
- **Monitoring**: Real-time performance metrics and alerting

### 2. Data Pipeline

A reliable data pipeline is crucial:

```python
def process_data(raw_data):
    # Clean and validate
    cleaned = validate_schema(raw_data)
    
    # Feature engineering
    features = extract_features(cleaned)
    
    return features
```

### 3. Model Versioning

Always version your models and maintain rollback capabilities.

## Conclusion

Building AI at scale is as much about engineering as it is about data science. The best models are useless without proper infrastructure.

---

*Thanks for reading! Feel free to reach out if you have questions.*

items.title: The Art of Product Thinking
items.excerpt: How I approach building products that users actually love...
items.author: Smrita Pandey
items.content:
## Thinking About Products

Product thinking is both an art and a science. When building products that serve real users, every decision matters.

## Core Principles

### Start With the User

Don't build what you think is cool — build what your users need. Talk to them, observe them, understand their pain points.

### Design for Failure

Everything fails eventually. Design your products to be resilient and forgiving.

### Measure Everything

What you can't measure, you can't improve.

## Common Patterns

- **User research**: Talking to real people before writing code
- **Rapid prototyping**: Getting something tangible fast
- **Iterative improvement**: Ship, learn, improve, repeat

## What I've Learned

1. **Simple beats clever** — Users don't care about your architecture
2. **Speed wins** — Ship fast, iterate faster
3. **Listen more** — Your users know best

*More on this topic coming soon...*

items.title: From Code to CEO
items.excerpt: My journey transitioning from writing code to building companies...
items.author: Smrita Pandey
items.content:
## The Journey

My path from writing code to building companies wasn't planned. It evolved naturally from solving problems I cared about.

## What Changed

As a developer, I focused on building great products. As a founder, I had to think about:

- Vision and strategy
- Team building
- Business development
- Customer relationships

## Lessons Learned

1. **Code is just one part** — The best product doesn't always win
2. **People matter most** — Your team makes or breaks you
3. **Stay technical** — Don't lose touch with the craft

## What's Next

I'm still learning every day. The journey continues...

---

*Share your own stories with me!*

items.title: If the Internet Was a Story, Would You Be the Hero or the Victim?
items.excerpt: Cybersecurity isn’t just technology. It’s human behavior, psychology, and storytelling.
items.author: Smrita Pandey
items.content:
# If the Internet Was a Story, Would You Be the Hero or the Victim?

When we think about cybersecurity, we imagine code.

Firewalls. Encryption. Hackers in hoodies.

But the truth is much simpler.

Cybersecurity is not a technology problem.

It’s a **human problem**.

---

## ✦ The uncomfortable reality

Most cyberattacks don’t happen because systems are weak.

They happen because **people are predictable**.

- We trust too easily  
- We click too fast  
- We don’t think twice  

And that’s exactly what attackers rely on.

---

## ✦ Let’s turn this into a story

Imagine this:

You’re the main character.

You get a message:
> “Your account has been compromised. Click here to secure it.”

What do you do?

---

## ✦ Quick Scenario Quiz

### Situation 1:
You receive an email from your “bank.”

It looks real.

Do you:
A. Click immediately  
B. Ignore it  
C. Verify the sender  
D. Panic and reset everything  

---

### Situation 2:
A friend sends you a suspicious link.

Do you:
A. Trust them and open it  
B. Ask them if they sent it  
C. Ignore it  
D. Report it  

---

### Situation 3:
You see a “limited offer” online.

Do you:
A. Buy instantly  
B. Research the site  
C. Check reviews  
D. Close it  

---

## ✦ Your result

If you chose mostly A or D:

You’re reactive.

And that’s exactly what attackers want.

If you chose mostly B or C:

You’re aware.

And awareness is your strongest defense.

---

## ✦ What I realized

While working on cybersecurity concepts, I noticed something interesting:

People don’t learn from lectures.

They learn from **stories**.

That’s why I once explored the idea of teaching cybersecurity through an **animated series**.

Because when you see a character get hacked, manipulated, or targeted—

You don’t just understand it.

You feel it.

---

## ✦ Why storytelling matters

Facts inform.

Stories transform.

A statistic won’t change behavior.

But a story might.

That’s why:
- cyberbullying feels “small” until you see its impact  
- privacy feels irrelevant until it’s violated  
- security feels optional until it’s too late  

---

## ✦ The real lesson

You don’t need to be a hacker to stay safe.

You need to:

- slow down  
- question things  
- think before acting  

Because in the digital world—

The biggest vulnerability is not your system.

It’s your **decision-making**.

---

## ✦ Final thought

Every time you go online, you’re part of a story.

The only question is:

Are you the one in control?

Or the one being controlled?

items.title: Your Brain Is Not Designed for Modern Life (And That’s the Problem)
items.excerpt: Working memory, attention, and cognitive bias are silently shaping your success.
items.author: Smrita Pandey
items.content:
# Your Brain Is Not Designed for Modern Life (And That’s the Problem)

You think you’re bad at focusing.

You think you procrastinate too much.

You think you’re not disciplined enough.

But what if the problem isn’t you?

What if it’s your brain?

---

## ✦ Let’s talk about working memory

Your brain can only hold a limited amount of information at once.

That’s called **working memory**.

And it’s constantly overloaded.

---

## ✦ Quick Experiment

Try this:

Remember this sequence:

7 - 2 - 9 - 4 - 1 - 8 - 3

Now reverse it.

Hard?

That’s your working memory limit.

---

## ✦ Now imagine this in real life

You’re:
- juggling assignments  
- checking notifications  
- thinking about the future  
- dealing with stress  

No wonder you feel overwhelmed.

---

## ✦ Attention is your real currency

Another thing I learned:

Attention is not infinite.

It’s selective.

And it’s fragile.

---

## ✦ Mini Quiz: Where is your attention going?

Right now, how often do you:

- Check your phone while studying?  
- Switch between tasks?  
- Get distracted mid-work?  

Be honest.

---

### If your answer is “a lot”:

You don’t have a motivation problem.

You have an **attention problem**.

---

## ✦ Cognitive biases: the hidden layer

Here’s where it gets interesting.

Your brain doesn’t just process information.

It distorts it.

Example:

**Anchoring bias** — you rely too much on the first piece of information.

This is used everywhere:
- marketing  
- social media  
- even cyberattacks  

---

## ✦ How this helped me

Understanding these concepts changed how I work.

Instead of forcing myself to “focus harder,” I:

- break tasks into smaller chunks  
- reduce distractions  
- design my environment intentionally  

I stopped fighting my brain.

I started working with it.

---

## ✦ The real takeaway

You don’t need more discipline.

You need better systems.

Because your brain:

- gets tired  
- gets distracted  
- takes shortcuts  

And that’s normal.

---

## ✦ Final thought

Your brain isn’t broken.

It’s just not designed for constant stimulation.

So instead of blaming yourself—

Design a life your brain can actually handle.

items.title: Don’t Think I Was Overthinking — I Just Didn’t Know What To Do With It
items.excerpt: A quiet reflection on overthinking, learning, and figuring out how to work with your own mind instead of against it.
items.author: Smrita Pandey
items.content:
# I Don’t Think I Was Overthinking — I Just Didn’t Know What To Do With It

I used to get told a lot that I overthink.

Not in a dramatic way. Just casually.

Like when I would take too long to decide something, or when I asked too many “why” questions about things that didn’t really need that much depth.

At some point, I just accepted it as a flaw.

Something to fix.

---

## ✦ It shows up in small ways

It’s not like I sit and think about big philosophical things all day.

It’s usually much smaller than that.

Like:
- replaying a conversation and wondering if I said something wrong  
- taking longer than necessary to start something because I want to understand it properly  
- getting distracted by trying to figure out how something works instead of just using it  

Nothing extreme. Just… constant.

---

## ✦ I thought the solution was to “think less”

For a while, I tried to be more like people who seemed more decisive.

Just do things. Don’t question everything.

And sometimes that worked.

But most of the time, it didn’t feel natural.

I would either:
- rush and feel unsatisfied  
- or end up going back and rethinking everything anyway  

---

## ✦ What changed

It wasn’t a big realization.

More like a gradual one.

While studying and working on different things — especially anything related to problem-solving or systems — I noticed that the same habit I was trying to suppress…

…was actually helping me.

When I slowed down and tried to understand:
- why something works  
- how different parts connect  
- what could go wrong  

I made fewer mistakes.

Not zero. Just fewer.

And I felt more confident in what I was doing.

---

## ✦ But it’s still not easy

I don’t think overthinking suddenly became a “strength.”

It still gets in the way.

Sometimes I spend too much time trying to understand something that didn’t need that much effort.

Sometimes I hesitate when I should just start.

And sometimes I get tired of my own thoughts.

---

## ✦ A small thing that helped

Instead of trying to stop overthinking completely, I started doing something simpler.

I separate:
- when I need to think  
- and when I need to act  

Not perfectly. Just intentionally.

For example:
If I’m starting something new, I give myself time to understand it properly.

But after a point, I stop researching and just begin, even if I don’t feel fully ready.

---

## ✦ I also stopped assuming everyone else has it figured out

This was a big one.

In college, especially, it’s easy to feel like other people are more confident or more clear about what they’re doing.

But when you talk to them, you realize most people are also:
- unsure  
- adjusting  
- figuring things out as they go  

Just in different ways.

---

## ✦ If you relate to this

I don’t think the answer is to become someone who never overthinks.

That probably won’t work.

Maybe it’s more about:

- noticing when it’s helping  
- noticing when it’s not  
- and slowly learning the difference  

It’s not a clean process.

---

## ✦ Final thought

I still think a lot.

Probably more than I need to.

But I don’t see it as something that needs to be completely fixed anymore.

Just something I’m learning how to manage.

And I think that’s enough for now.

items.title: Why I Build Things Nobody Asked For
items.excerpt: The best projects I've worked on were never assigned to me. They started with a question I couldn't stop thinking about.
items.author: Smrita Pandey
items.content:
## The pattern I didn't notice at first

No one told me to build an OCR system for SRCC. No one asked me to design a GRC compliance platform. No one assigned NeurQ AI as a project requirement.

I just started building.

Looking back, I realize every project I've worked on followed the same cognitive pattern: I noticed a problem, I got curious about solving it, I couldn't stop thinking about it, and I started building. No brief. No client. No deadline. Just curiosity — and the inability to leave a problem unsolved.

---

## The psychology behind it

![The Zeigarnik Effect — unfinished projects demanding attention](/img/blog/inline-zeigarnik.png)


In cognitive psychology, there's a concept called the **Zeigarnik Effect** — the tendency for people to remember incomplete tasks better than completed ones. Once my brain latched onto a problem, it kept running in the background, demanding resolution. I didn't understand this at first. I just thought I was restless.

As I reflected on what I've learned from studying cognitive psychology, I realized the knowledge and concepts have already started to shape my perspective and understanding of various aspects of life — including how I approach building. Understanding the limitations of working memory helped me appreciate the importance of breaking down complex problems into smaller, manageable chunks. By doing so, I reduce the cognitive load, which allows me to approach my work with more focus and clarity.

---

## Why self-initiated projects matter

The projects that taught me the most were never the ones assigned to me in a classroom. They were the ones I chose. When you choose the problem, you own the outcome — every failure, every breakthrough. There's no external deadline to blame or syllabus to follow. You have to figure out what to learn, when to pivot, and when to stop.

As a college student, I often juggle multiple assignments, deadlines, and responsibilities. But the self-initiated projects — the ones born from genuine curiosity rather than a syllabus — always demanded a different kind of attention. When you're intrinsically motivated, your attentional resources are naturally directed toward the task. You don't need willpower to focus. The curiosity does the work for you.

---

## The downside

Not every project ships. Some die in a folder called "experiments." Some get halfway done and abandoned when a newer, shinier problem appears. That's the Zeigarnik Effect working against me — the next incomplete task pulls my attention away from the current one.

But I've learned that's okay. The point was never to finish everything. The point was to learn. And every abandoned experiment left behind knowledge that showed up later in something that did ship.

---

## What I'd tell someone starting out

Keep building things nobody asked for. Don't wait for permission or a course requirement to start solving problems. Because someday, someone will need exactly what you built — and you'll already know how to build it.

The best portfolio isn't a collection of assignments. It's a collection of curiosities.

items.title: The Gap Between Learning and Doing
items.excerpt: I've watched hundreds of tutorials and still felt like I couldn't build anything on my own. Understanding why changed everything.
items.author: Smrita Pandey
items.content:
## The illusion of competence

I've watched hundreds of tutorials. Read countless documentation pages. Followed step-by-step guides that walked me through building entire applications.

And still felt like I couldn't build anything on my own.

If that sounds familiar, there's actually a cognitive explanation for it.

---

## What's really happening in your brain

In cognitive psychology, this is related to the difference between **recognition** and **recall**. When you follow a tutorial, you're exercising recognition — you see the code, it makes sense, you understand the logic. Your brain registers this as "I know this."

But building from scratch requires recall — pulling information from memory without cues. These are fundamentally different cognitive processes, and one doesn't automatically lead to the other. This is why you can watch someone build an entire React app and then stare at a blank VS Code screen with no idea where to start.

I experienced this firsthand during my studies at ASU. The lectures made perfect sense. The textbook examples were clear. But when I sat down to apply the concepts independently — whether in cybersecurity labs or coding projects — there was always a gap between what I understood and what I could actually do.

---

## The tutorial trap

![Watching vs Building — the gap between passive learning and active doing](/img/blog/inline-tutorial.png)

![Watching vs Building — the gap between learning and doing](/img/blog/inline-tutorial.png)


Tutorials give you the illusion of progress. You watch someone build something, and your brain thinks *you* built it. But you didn't. You watched. And the encoding that happens during passive consumption is shallow compared to the deep encoding that happens during active problem-solving.

This is consistent with what I learned about working memory limitations. When you follow a tutorial, the tutorial is doing the heavy cognitive lifting for you — planning the architecture, sequencing the steps, debugging the errors. Your working memory isn't being used to its full capacity. So when the tutorial is gone, your working memory doesn't know how to structure the problem.

---

## What actually bridges the gap

The only thing that works is **building something without a guide**. It doesn't have to be original. It just has to be yours. Pick a project slightly above your current level. Get stuck. Search furiously for answers. Figure it out. Repeat.

When I built the SRCC OCR system, I had no tutorial to follow. I just had a problem — digitizing documents — and started solving it. I broke things constantly. But every error taught me more than any course module ever did. Because when something broke, my entire attentional system was engaged in understanding why.

---

## The uncomfortable truth

You will never feel ready. You will never know enough. Start anyway.

Because the gap between learning and doing only closes when you start doing. And the errors you make along the way? Those are the real lessons. Your brain encodes failure far more deeply than success.

items.title: What Anime Taught Me About Resilience and Communication
items.excerpt: From A Silent Voice to Naruto, anime has shaped how I think about perseverance, empathy, and building things that matter.
items.author: Smrita Pandey
items.content:
## This might sound unconventional

When people ask what shaped my perspective on communication, empathy, and perseverance, they probably expect me to reference a business book or a TED talk.

But honestly? A lot of it came from anime.

---

## A Silent Voice and the weight of communication

![Inspired by A Silent Voice — communication bridging the gap](/img/blog/koe-no-katachi.png)

![Communication bridging the gap — inspired by A Silent Voice](/img/blog/inline-silent-voice.png)


One of the most impactful films I've studied is *A Silent Voice*, directed by Naoko Yamada. On the surface, it's a story about bullying and redemption. But at its core, it's a profound exploration of how communication shapes human connection — and what happens when it fails.

Shouko, the deaf protagonist, was able to overcome her shortcomings by depending upon vibrations and expressions, as shown throughout the movie through cinematographical cues such as visual and auditory cues. What struck me most was how the film demonstrates that communication isn't just about words or hearing — it's about the willingness to understand someone different from you.

Chizuru's character is a good example of a girl challenging gender stereotypes. She prefers to be perceived as a boy not because she is transgender or tomboyish — instead, she uses a boy's gender as a shell to protect her sister. She photographs dead creatures' corpses to show Shouko the dark side of death because she wants Shouko to not attempt suicide. She started doing this when young Shouko said she wanted to die after being bullied so much at school. Chizuru is a strong girl and shows us how to be brave when faced with adversity.

The movie also shows us how women can challenge gender stereotypes through Sahara's character. She was harassed by her classmates for her sense of fashion but she grew up to be strong enough to face her bullies, going so far as to take up fashion electives in high school. She even befriended her worst bully, Ueno. All the female cast gives the impression to the viewer that it is not easy being a girl but you need to face your problems head-on.

---

## The underdog archetype

![Determination against all odds — inspired by Rock Lee](/img/blog/rocklee-determination.png)

Then there's the classic anime pattern that I keep coming back to:

- **Naruto** — no natural talent, all determination
- **Midoriya** — born powerless in a world that values power
- **Rock Lee** — unable to use ninjutsu, becomes the most skilled fighter through pure effort

The pattern is always the same: someone with no natural advantage outworks everyone through sheer stubbornness. This resonates with me because I didn't start coding early. I didn't have a computer science background in my family. But I had one thing: I refused to stop.

---

## The cognitive connection

What's interesting is how this connects to what I've studied in cognitive psychology. The concept of **cognitive load** suggests that persistence isn't just about willpower — it's about managing your mental resources effectively.

Rock Lee doesn't just train more. He trains *differently*. He recognizes his limitation and builds an entirely new system around it. That's not just motivation — that's strategic cognitive adaptation.

---

## Why I proposed an animated cybersecurity series

This understanding of anime's power to communicate complex ideas is exactly why I once proposed creating an animated series about cybersecurity awareness. The goal was to target people from all over the world because the problem of cybersecurity concerns every person on the internet directly or indirectly.

People don't learn from lectures. They learn from stories. Just telling teenagers about online safety might only help a little, but if a longlasting effect is desired, showing them through media and realistic examples might be better. A YouTube channel called Common Sense Education has a few videos educating teens about digital footprint — using teen voices to educate other teens with real-life examples. It successfully spread the lesson they wanted to teach. However, the videos are not popular and a teen rarely comes across them.

Therefore, creating a web series with characters teenagers could identify with — storylines that demonstrate social engineering, privacy violations, and online predators in context — could reach people in a way that security training never could. Because when you see a character get hacked, manipulated, or targeted, you don't just understand the threat. You feel it.

---

## The most important message

The final message from A Silent Voice can be interpreted as that women have the ability to fight against all odds. The most important message is that communication is vital for a healthy society. Facing society as a man or a woman is not avoidable, and resorting to giving up is not the answer. If you are in need, seek help from your family and friends because no matter the differences between you and your peers, we are all human — therefore we can learn to accept those differences and support each other regardless.

Anime taught me that the most powerful stories are the ones that make you feel something you can't un-feel. And that's not just storytelling — that's cognitive science. We remember emotional experiences more deeply than factual ones. Stories transform. Facts just inform.

items.title: Designing for the Distracted Mind
items.excerpt: Most users don't notice good design. They only notice bad design. Understanding attention and working memory changed how I build interfaces.
items.author: Smrita Pandey
items.content:
## A truth most designers don't want to hear

Your users don't care about your design. They care about getting things done. They don't want your clever animations. They want to find the button.

This sounds harsh, but it's grounded in everything I've learned about attention and cognitive load.

---

## What cognitive psychology tells us about users

One of the key topics that has had a significant impact on my understanding of design is Working Memory. Working memory is the cognitive system responsible for temporarily holding and manipulating information necessary for complex cognitive tasks. Understanding the limitations of working memory has helped me appreciate the importance of effective organization — not just in daily tasks, but in interface design.

As a college student, I often juggle multiple assignments, deadlines, and responsibilities. I now recognize the significance of breaking down complex information into smaller, manageable chunks. By doing so, I reduce the cognitive load on working memory, which allows users to approach tasks with more focus and clarity. This simple realization has implications far beyond academics — it fundamentally changes how I design software.

The concept of working memory has practical implications for cybersecurity too. In this field, it is crucial to manage and process a vast amount of information in real-time to detect and respond to security threats effectively. The design of user interfaces and security dashboards that take into account the limitations of working memory can greatly enhance the performance of cybersecurity professionals.

---

## Attention and consciousness in design

![How the brain selectively focuses on interface elements](/img/blog/inline-ux-brain.png)


Another topic I found particularly relevant is Attention and Consciousness. This delves into the mechanisms of attention, selective focus, and conscious awareness. Learning about the different types of attention and strategies to enhance focus has been transformative for how I build interfaces.

I now employ techniques informed by attention research when designing:

- **Selective attention**: Users will focus on what seems most relevant to their goal. Make the primary action obvious.
- **Divided attention**: People are terrible at processing multiple information streams simultaneously. Don't put competing calls-to-action on the same screen.
- **Sustained attention**: Users get fatigued. Long forms, dense dashboards, and information-heavy pages lead to errors.

In my future career in cybersecurity, the concept of attention has direct implications for threat detection and incident response. Cybersecurity professionals need to maintain intense focus and vigilance to identify and respond to potential threats. Understanding how attention can be directed, sustained, and switched between tasks helps in building effective security monitoring systems.

---

## Cognitive biases in design

One common cognitive bias is the **anchoring bias**, where people tend to rely too heavily on the first piece of information they encounter when making decisions. In an ethical hacking competition, I utilized this concept by creating false clues that served as anchors for the defenders. The same principle applies to design — the first thing a user sees on your interface sets their expectation for everything else.

---

## A lesson from building products

When I built the GRC platform, I spent weeks perfecting the interface. Gradients. Glassmorphism. Smooth transitions. It looked beautiful.

Then someone asked: "Where's the export button?"

That moment taught me something critical: **aesthetics and usability are not the same thing.** The cognitive load I was adding with visual complexity was actually making the tool harder to use.

---

## The balance

I still love making things beautiful. The kawaii theme on this very blog is evidence of that. But beauty should serve usability, not compete with it.

The two questions I ask first:

1. Can the user find what they need in 3 seconds?
2. Does the design get out of the way?

If the answer to either is no, the design has failed — no matter how good it looks.

---

## Final thought

Design for the person who's tired, distracted, and in a hurry. Because that's what your brain is most of the time — overloaded, fatigued, and running on limited attentional resources. By incorporating principles from cognitive science, we can build interfaces that work *with* the human brain rather than against it.

items.title: Faraway Memories
items.excerpt: A girl wakes up in complete darkness with no memory of who she is. A golden void, holographic screens, and a mysterious meadow await her. Chapter 1 & 2 of an original story.
items.author: Smrita Pandey
items.content:
# CHAPTER 1

Beep

Beep

Beep–

In complete darkness, an eerie voice echoed through the void and a girl was lying motionless in that emptiness. Is she dead? Is this the afterlife? The void that awaits us beyond the end of our lives?

Her eyes stirred for a moment, and she jolted awake with clenched fists and a panting breath. She strained her eyes to see something, anything… yet was unable to perceive anything with either of her five senses. There was complete silence, no up or down, no ground beneath her feet, yet she could see herself faintly. She was standing yet there was no ground, as if floating in outer space.

“What is this place? Where am I?”

She slowly regained her cognition, looking all over herself, trying to make sense of this situation to no avail.

“I feel… I am… I am forgetting something important… Ugh–”

*M-my head hurts.*

As soon as that thought passed her mind, she belatedly discovered that she didn't know who she was. She had no recollection of her life yet a deep pain pierced her heart as if she had lost something precious to her, more than her life. Tears streamed down her face from the pain in her chest and she slowly tried stepping forward.

At the moment her toe tried to make a step in the nothingness, rings of golden light rippled at once and gradually lit up the area around her. The reverberating light, as if ripples in a pond made by a drop of water spread as far as she could see and the whole space lit up with a bright golden glow. She had to squint her eyes as she was blinded by the sudden brightness for her pupils to adjust to the transformation.

She slowly advanced a few more steps, hesitance evident in each step. She could now clearly feel the ground and see what was in front of her. Dozens of holographic screens were floating all around her. On closer inspection, she could see that all the screens were blurry except for one which was right in front of her. She approached that screen in a daze, as if it was calling out to her and, before she realized it, she was reaching out her hand as if her body was moving on its own.

“What is happening? My… M-my hand is passing through the screen?!”

She was surprised out of her daze by the sudden pull but before she could think any further, she was already sucked into the hologram. She shut her eyes tightly as she was pulled by an unknown force and found herself inside the screen. She jarred her eyes wide to see that she was now in a completely different location that looked exactly the same as the scenery portrayed in the holograph she was swallowed by.

She found herself under a tree on a small hill that overlooked a beautiful lush green meadow. The tree had a swing hanging from its lowest branch and a gentle breeze flowing through the land pushed the swing, giving it a slight momentum to harmonically sway with the wind.

The scene was so mesmerizing that she lost all strength in her knees and slid to the ground. With a dumbfounded look in her wide eyes, she looked up to the sky and saw a dandelion floating towards her. She reached out her hand and the dandelion lightly perched on her wrist. A smile broke out on her dumbstruck face and she felt a rush of nostalgia as if this was a familiar, everyday sight. She delicately picked up the bloom and put it atop her palm before blowing it away with her breath. The dandelion picked up the wind again and flew past her this time as she looked over her shoulder and followed its motion with her eyes. Her eyes then landed on a distant hut behind her and she felt compelled to rush to that place.

However, she suppressed her urge and sat down in a more comfortable position before inhaling and exhaling a few times, breathing deeply with a sigh.

“Calm down. Phew–”

She tried to organize her thoughts to try and understand what was happening.

“Let me see… I don't remember my name and who I was before waking up in the darkness. I don't remember anything. Nothing makes sense. I was in a completely white room that stretched infinitely on all sides and there were blurry screens. Did I perhaps… die? Is this another world? But… why does it seem so familiar?”

*Wait wait wait wai–*

Her heart suddenly started beating wildly and her lips parted as she panted as if asphyxiated. She lost her balance and fell unconscious on the grass under the tree's shade.

---

# CHAPTER 2

Yet again, she woke up, adjusting her eyes to her surroundings. This time too, she found herself in an unfamiliar room. She sat up on the bed she was lying in and looked out the window beside the small bed. The landscape she had witnessed before fainting was still as tranquil as she remembered. Perhaps, she was inside the hut she had glanced at earlier.

“Ah… you woke up already? Here, young lady”

An old man, maybe around her grandfather's age, walked in. He had a tray in his hand that had a glass of water. He offered it to her, a slight smile on his wrinkled face. Her brain wanted to hesitate but her parched body moved on its own, similar to how it has been acting since she woke up in this strange place. While gulping it down, she kept thinking that her body was acting quite strange, as if it was being moved by someone else's will. She had no memories yet everything felt strangely familiar.

She cleared her throat and kept the glass on the side table. She had so many questions and her train of thought was running wild with anxiety. She decided to inquire the old man.

“Hello sir, who are you?”

“I am the owner of this hut.”

*Yeah, that answers nothing.*

“Sir, where is this place?”

“That is a question you'll have to find the answer to, yourself.”

His roundabout answers started to irritate the girl but she suppressed her annoyance. She chose her next words carefully.

“How did I end up here?”

“You were sleeping under that tree on the hill, Ygg saw you and brought you here.” He said, pointing to the tree visible from the window.

“Ygg? And uh… I was not sleeping there… uh… I meant to ask how did I come to this place?”

“Ygg carried you from that place to the bed.”

*Who even is Ygg? Can this person not not make sense?*

Frustrated by his simple words and nonchalant tone, she decided to try asking one last time.

“No, I mean thank you for carrying me all the way but, how did I come to this meadow?”

“How would I know that?”

*Okay, that's it. I better stop expecting proper answers.*

“I see…………”

From the corner of my eye, a glimpse of black blur flittered past the door and a little fur ball was now tugging onto the old man's leg.

“Oh ygg is here to say hello”

*That is Ygg?! The one who carried me here? How is that possible?!*

---
